data.title: Health And Happiness: Positive On Growth Prospects And Capital Allocation (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've become a bull on Health And Happiness International (OTCPK:BTSDF) (1112.HK) or "HAHI". The previous October 23, 2024 update looked at various factors influencing BTSDF's future results.

HAHI is deserving of a Buy now in my opinion. This considers the ANAC (Adult Nutrition And Care) segment's improved outlook and its favorable capital management moves. In China, there is increased public awareness about "EGT or Ergothioneine" and the potential effects of anti-aging supplements. This is likely to drive up sales of BTSDF's adult nutrition offerings.

Stock Price Outperformance Draws Attention To Adult Nutrition Unit's Growth Prospects

The group's Hong Kong-listed (primary listing) shares jumped by 40% in the past month.

Due to capital controls, Mainland Chinese (MC) are only allowed to purchase selected HK (Hong Kong) shares via a scheme called "Southbound Stock Connect". HAHI's Southbound fund inflows as a proportion of its free float expanded by a substantial 12 ppts (percentage points) in May 2025 based on S&P Capital IQ data. That should have been the key driver of its price surge.

The company has recently become one of the most favored investments among PRC (People's Republic Of China) investors, for good reasons.

An early May Futubull news report highlighted that a septuagenarian (also chairperson of a China health business) maintained a "muscular physique" by taking supplements containing EGT (Ergothioneine). The ingredient is described as a "longevity vitamin" by Nature.com. Local investors would have likely searched for stocks leveraged to this theme and found HAHI.

BTSDF's ANAC division has its own ergothioneine offering called "Swisse Plus Advanced Cellular Renewal". Its product sales could rise meaningfully going forward, as PRC's consumers get more interested in healthcare solutions, especially those related to anti-aging.

HAHI earned 73 percent of its FY24 EBITDA from the adult nutrition business. The MC region contributed 65% of this ANAC unit's topline last year. BTSDF also boasts a market-leading 8% share of China's VMS (Vitamins & Mineral Supplements) sales in the digital channel. These figures sourced from its corporate slides suggest that the stock is a play on growing health awareness in the PRC.

The flagship supplement brand for BTSDF is "Swisse". ANAC has developed multiple sub-brands over time. "Little Swisse", "Swisse Me", and "Swisse Plus" are targeted at kids, teens, and wealthier middle-aged individuals, respectively. The final one, which has the EGT product "Advanced Cellular Renewal" in its line-up, boasts the strongest potential.

SPB (Swisse Plus Brand) was first launched in late 2022 and has grown to account for more than 10% of the group's FY2024 adult nutrition sales. In the prior year, SPB's revenue increased by one-fifth, which was much faster than the ANAC segment's overall growth of +7%.

It's reasonable to assume that "Swisse Plus" boasts higher ASPs (Average Selling Prices) as a premium sub-brand. HAHI's group-level profitability is expected to improve in the future, as revenue from the higher-margin SPB rises. My view is that adding new anti-aging solutions to the "Swisse Plus" portfolio will be a simple way of driving up PRC sales.

I project that BTSDF's normalized earnings will increase at a +21% CAGR for the FY2024-2027 period.

My bottomline estimates have taken into account SPB's more rapid growth and superior margins (compared with the overall ANAC division). I anticipate that the company's revenue expansion on an annualized basis will accelerate from +4.2% for FY21-24 to +6.0% in the next three years. My forecasts also point to its net margin improving by +2ppts between FY2024 and FY2027.

Reduced Finance Expenses And Greater Dividend Certainty Are Major Positives

Business performance isn't the sole stock selection criteria. You would also want your investment candidate to be shareholder-friendly and less susceptible to rate environment changes. BTSDF has addressed these issues to a considerable degree with its guidance.

At its earlier FY24 analyst briefing, HAHI outlined its expectations of incurring normalized (adjusted for hedging effects) interest costs of CNY 0.56B (mid-point of guide) in the full year 2025.

This implies that the company's finance-related expenses could decrease by 14% this year. It also translates into a potential interest margin (interest divided by debt) improvement of 50 bps (basis points).

In January this year, BTSDF refinanced its $300M senior notes with new debt that carries a 4.4ppts lower coupon rate. The probability of HAHI meeting its guide has increased with this move.

A decline in funding costs will also enhance the group's bottomline.

Separately, the company has made a commitment (source: FY2024 results call) to deliver at least RMB 0.2 billion of cash distributions every year for FY25-27. This is roughly the same as its actual 2024 dividends, amounting to CNY 206M.

A lot of listed corporations choose to define their shareholder return policy as a percentage of earnings. I don't think this is ideal for income-focused investors, as it means that future payouts will vary with earnings.

Instead, you are largely assured of a minimum 2.5% annual yield for the coming years, if one invests in HAHI at current prices. I'm sure many people will favor BTSDF's approach of setting a dividend floor.

Risks You Should Watch

Demand for supplements or vitamins in the PRC might weaken, if there is a "health scare" involving fake or poor-quality products in the industry.

HAHI might leave investors disappointed if it falls short of its targeted interest savings and dividends.

My Closing Remarks

I'm lifting my rating for BTSDF from Hold to Buy.

I like the prospects of its core ANAC business and the way it manages its capital. As more consumers in the PRC become convinced of the effects of EGT and other longevity-related supplements, HAHI's adult nutrition product revenues should grow at a faster pace.

The company's shares are still cheap, notwithstanding the recent strong price performance.

HAHI's current PEG (Price-to-Earnings Growth) is an undemanding 0.6x (12.6/21). This is derived from my three-year profit CAGR forecast of 21 percent mentioned above and its 12.6 times (source: S&P Capital IQ) consensus NTM (Next Twelve Months) P/E.

A general rule is that a stock's fair valuation is 1xPEG.

Assuming I value HAHI at a more conservative 0.8 times Price-to-Earnings Growth, its upside is still an attractive +33% (0.8/0.6).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dollar Tree: Returning To Healthy Growth
data.description:
Dollar Tree, Inc. (NASDAQ:DLTR) reported quite a strong start to FY2025, as Temu’s issues have returned traffic to the discount retailer with suddenly improved discretionary product sales. Despite a turbulent consumer environment and the tariff landscape, Dollar Tree even slightly raised the FY2025 EPS guidance. With the underwhelming Family Dollar segment being sold, Dollar Tree is returning to a well-performing company.

I remained at a cautious Hold rating in my previous October 2024 article on the stock, titled “Dollar Tree: Waiting For A Family Dollar Strategic Decision.” The stock has since returned 30%, well above the S&P 500’s (SP500) return of 2%.

Dollar Tree’s Q1 Reflects Gains from Temu’s Losses

Dollar Tree’s fiscal Q1 report from the February-April period underlines a similar theme to Dollar General Corporation (DG), while the stock reaction was quite the opposite at a -8% decline. Revenues reached $4.64 billion with 11.3% total growth, reflecting Dollar Tree’s continued store expansion and a very noteworthy improvement in comparable store sales to 5.4% from just 1.7% in FY2024. The sales result exceeded Wall Street’s consensus by $108 million.

The comparable store sales improvement is great, especially as the result was achieved in a turbulent consumer environment and tariff landscape. Underneath, the current tariff on China has created a favorable competitive shift as I also noted in my article on Dollar General’s earnings – PDD Holdings Inc. (PDD) has struggled with its Temu platform in the U.S. after tariff implementation, now even hiding Chinese listings from U.S. consumers while trying to adapt its supply chain for tariffs. Temu’s estimated U.S. growth decelerated from 50% growth at the start of April to a -30% decline in May, and Dollar Tree’s traffic clearly gained from the ecommerce competitor’s issues. Dollar Tree’s slowly declining discretionary product sales turned into a 4.6% comparable store sales growth in Q1, as the company regained lost discretionary sales from Temu.

Dollar Tree’s profitability was still relatively weak in Q1, as a 20 basis point gain in the gross margin was more than offset by a 140 basis point margin pressure in operating income. Total operating income came in at $384 million, up by just 0.6% year-on-year as the operating margin declined to 8.3%. Higher payroll expenses, general liability claims, utilities, and depreciation caused the margin to decrease. Wage increases seem likely to cause further earnings pressure throughout FY2025. The quarter’s adjusted EPS of $1.26 still beat Wall Street’s consensus by $0.05.

Notably, Dollar Tree still slightly raised the FY2025 guidance. The revenue range was reaffirmed at $18.5-19.1 billion, suggesting 7% growth at the mid-point with 3-5% comparable store sales growth, but Dollar Tree raised the adjusted EPS guidance range by fifteen cents to $5.15-5.65 to reflect recent share repurchases. The guidance suggests fair earnings growth from a $5.10 adjusted EPS in FY2024. In a very turbulent operating environment, highlighted by weak consumer confidence and the current 30% tariff on China that causes noteworthy sourcing price pressure on Dollar Tree, the raised guidance sends quite a strong signal.

The Q2 report is expected to show a steep but temporary earnings drop – Dollar Tree anticipates the sales environment to be healthy for the quarter, but the massive 145% tariff on China for a brief period caused Dollar Tree massive excess costs in sourcing as told in the Q1 earnings call. The second quarter’s EPS is expected to be nearly half. Ultimately, the Q2 earnings drop will be transitory, as Dollar Tree still guides for healthy earnings for FY2025 in the current tariff landscape. Dollar Tree even expects the full-year gross margin to improve by 50-75 basis points, as the company appears to be able to mitigate tariff pressure well with global sourcing.

Family Dollar Is Being Sold

As a conclusion to Dollar Tree’s strategic review for the underperforming Family Dollar segment, the company announced to be selling the segment back in March. The transaction is expected to close in early summer as the transaction has already received regulatory approval. Family Dollar is being sold for a low consideration of $1007 million, compared to the 2015 valuation of approximately $9.2 billion upon the segment’s acquisition.

Family Dollar’s continued brand underperformance makes the low consideration an expected outcome. While underlining a decade-long disappointing performance, the transaction positively returns Dollar Tree into a well-performing company.

Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model to account for Dollar Tree’s Family Dollar sale and the updated earnings outlook. Based on Dollar Tree’s continued store expansion and good FY2025 guidance, I now estimate a good FY2024-FY2034 revenue CAGR of 4.5%. I estimate stable 2% perpetual growth after the estimate period.

Dollar Tree’s margin performance is pressured by increased operating expenses, but the company’s upbeat sales outlook and good gross margin guidance offset a good portion of the weakness. I estimate Dollar Tree’s EBIT margin to stabilize at 8.5%. The company’s cash flow conversion is weakened by continued store expansion, but Dollar Tree should regardless continue to generate healthy free cash flow. I expect the cash flow conversion to slowly improve as store openings slow down.

I have added $1007 million of cash to Dollar Tree’s balance sheet from the Family Dollar divestment.

The estimates put Dollar Tree’s fair value at $100.32, 13% above the stock price at the time of writing. With quite a good forward outlook, the stock has a moderate upside. I believe that the estimated upside represents a fair margin of safety in the investment.

The fair value estimate is up from $84.84 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.62%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a low 2.65% interest rate, annualized from Q1 interest expenses and debt. I again assume a modest 10% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.36% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.86. With a liquidity premium of 0.1%, the cost of equity stands at 8.18% and the WACC at 7.62%.

Takeaway

Dollar Tree’s Q1 performance gained from Temu’s weakness, as discretionary item sales improved sharply. Despite an uncertain consumer environment and tariff pressure on especially Chinese imports, Dollar Tree even slightly raised the FY2025 EPS guidance, underlining good resilience in a challenging operating environment. The company’s earnings outlook is now quite good following the sale of Family Dollar. As DLTR stock is valued with a fair margin of safety, I remain with a Hold rating.

data.title: Harmony Biosciences: If It Ain't Broke
data.description:
Today, I put the biopharma name Harmony Biosciences Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:HRMY) in the spotlight. The last time I looked at Harmony in August of last year, I recommended the stock for a small, covered call position. That turned out to be a profitable trade. The company reported quarterly results that slightly beat top and bottom-line expectations a month ago. Management also projected its flagship product would achieve over $1 billion in annual sales by FY2030. Therefore, it seems a good time to circle back to this small-cap biopharma concern. An updated analysis of Harmony Biosciences follows below.

Harmony Biosciences Holdings, Inc. is headquartered in Pennsylvania. The company's flagship drug is WAKIX (pitolisant). This is a molecule with a novel mechanism of action for the treatment of excessive daytime sleepiness or EDS in adult patients with narcolepsy. It was approved for the treatment of EDS in narcolepsy in August 2019. This was followed by FDA approval for the treatment of cataplexy in adult patients with narcolepsy in October 2020. Other products approved for indications in narcolepsy include Xyrem/Xywav from Jazz Pharmaceuticals plc (JAZZ) as well as LUMRYZ from Avadel Pharmaceuticals plc (AVDL). There are just over 75,000 individuals diagnosed with narcolepsy in the United States. The stock trades around $35.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $2 billion.

The company has several other candidates in development within its pipeline. These include efforts to extend the patent protection of its WAKIX franchise by getting improved versions of the compound approved. This is important as currently WAKIX has potential patent protection issues. The company reached a settlement this week with another generic drugmaker to prevent their entry into the market until 2030. Improved versions if approved could give Harmony market exclusivity until 2044. The company plans to kick off pivotal trials for both WAKIX's two approved indications in the fourth quarter for this upgraded version of WAKIX called Pitolisant HD (high-dose) in the fourth quarter of this year. If all goes to schedule, this should result in FDA approvals sometime in 2028.

The company also launched a bioequivalence study for Pitolisant GR (gastro-resistant) in the first quarter of this year. Top-line results from this trial are projected to come out in the third quarter of this year with a potential PDUFA date in 2026. The company has filed utility patents on this version of pitolisant out to 2044. An article in late 2024 here on Seeking Alpha projected FDA approval could eventually trigger $300 million to $500 million of peak sales potential.

Harmony has two other late-stage efforts underway as well. One is with a candidate called ZYN002, which is a cannabidiol (CBD) gel product. The company got this asset when it purchased Zynerba Pharmaceuticals late in 2023. This candidate has the potential to be the first and only approved treatment for patients with Fragile X Syndrome or FXS, which impacts approximately 80,000 people in the U.S. Top-line data from a registrational Phase 3 study should be out in the third quarter of this year.

Harmony also has a 5HT2 (serotonin) agonist candidate called EPX-100. The company is currently recruiting subjects for two Phase 3 registrational trials, one for Dravet's Syndrome and the other for Lennox-Gastaut syndrome. Both trials should see top-line data come out sometime in 2026, partially dependent on enrollment for these studies are complete.

Recent Results:

Harmony Biosciences Holdings posted Q1 numbers on May 6th. The company delivered non-GAAP profits of $1.02 a share, 13 cents a share above expectations. Net income was up 19% from the same period a year ago to $45.6 million. Operating expenses did grow 29% to $96.5 million, the majority of this was driven by increases in R&D to move the candidates in the company's pipeline forward.

Revenues rose 19.5% on a year-over-year basis to $184.7 million. Sales growth was driven by organic demand as WAKIX has nearly 10% market share based on narcolepsy-impacted individuals in the U.S. Management provided FY2025 revenue guidance of between $820 million to $860 million. At its midpoint, this would imply revenue growth of 17.5% over FY2024.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

The analyst firm community is generally positive about the prospects around Harmony Biosciences Holdings at the moment. Since first quarter results hit the wires, four analyst firms including Oppenheimer and Needham have reissued Buy ratings on the stock. Price targets proffered range from $38 to $70 a share. Bank of America ($31 price target, down from $32 previously) maintained its Sell rating on the shares.

Just over four percent of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. Several insiders have sold shares for approximately $4.5 million collectively so far in 2025. The company ended Q1 with just over $610 million of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet, an increase of just over $34 million from the end of the previous quarter. The company listed just under $160 million in long-term debt on the 10-K it filed for Q1.

Conclusion:

Harmony Biosciences Holdings made $4.04 a share (non-GAAP) in FY2024 on just under $715 million in revenues. The current analyst firm consensus has earnings increasing slightly to FY2025 to $4.18 a share on $841 million in sales. They project $5.42 a share in profits on revenue growth in the high teens in FY2026. That leaves the stock trading at under nine times trailing earnings, a huge discount to the valuation of the S&P 500.

I concluded my last article on Harmony Biosciences in the following way while recommending it for a covered call trade given the options against the equity are liquid and the option premiums are solid:

The company is profitable, is seeing consistent sales growth from WAKIX, has a solid balance sheet, and has some potential growth drivers on the horizon. The stock has had a solid floor at just below $30 a share throughout 2024."

As my late father liked to quip 'If it ain't broken, don't fix it'. Therefore, HRMY still makes an attractive covered call trade. And this is how I have once again accumulated a position in the stock this week.

data.title: Amazon: Investors Wanted Pure Play AWS, And Now They're Getting It
data.description:
• Note: This was written and published while Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) traded at $205/share. The math has marginally shifted, though the thrust of my thoughts remains the same.

Finally, We Can Buy AWS As A Standalone Business

The central argument I will convey to you today is that buying Amazon has become buying pure play Amazon Web Services, or AWS.

Over the last decade, countless investors have asked for Amazon to spin off AWS such that it could be bought as a standalone business, and, to be sure, that would have been an incredible opportunity for investors; however, it's never come to pass.

That said, due to some of the most astonishing valuation dynamics I think I've ever seen in the stock market, i.e., Walmart Inc. (WMT) trading at least, 33% premium to Amazon, today, we're able to buy ticker symbol AMZN in such a way that we're only paying for AWS and getting the rest of the business totally free.

I will delineate the Walmart valuation comparison math towards the end of this review. But before I do that, let's walk through the math underlying my assertion that in buying ticker symbol AMZN today, we're only paying for AWS.

In some sense, this isn't even my contention. This is merely what the market has come to believe, and it is now affording us the incredible opportunity to buy AWS as a de facto standalone business.

The Math Of AWS

As I've already noted, I believe we're buying AWS as a pure play at ~$2T in market cap today, which is where Amazon trades approximately.

This implies that Amazon's ecommerce, logistics and fulfillment services, prime, ads, and future businesses, such as Kuiper (Starlink competitor), are worth $0.

I believe $2T is a fair value for AWS, in short, because it's an incredibly high quality, high margin, and oligopolistic business, with a giant runway for growth still ahead.

...What's interesting in AI is that we still haven't gotten to all the other customer experiences that are going to be reinvented and all the other agents that are going to be built, they're going to take the role of a lot of different functions today. And even though we have a lot of combined inference in those areas, I would say we're not even at the second strike of the first batter in the first inning. It is so early right now.

Andy Jassy, Amazon Q1 '25 Earnings Call (emphasis added).

To granularly understand the value of AWS, let's consider the business through the lens of the four points of stock value:

  1. The amount of free cash flow per share a business generates (We need to understand margins and share count trends to fully nail this down). Presently, AWS generates ~$117B in annualized sales with ~40% operating margins, and it has ~10.9B shares outstanding.
  2. The growth of that free cash flow per share (We need to understand TAM). In a moment, I will share a handful of data points that illustrate that AWS' growth runway spans decades, and the business should, over time, do hundreds of billions in revenue, if not $500B+.
  3. The durability of that free cash flow per share (We need to understand economic moats and competitive advantages that create defensible and durable cash flows). AWS is a mission-critical platform that's more like a utility than a "tech company." It's more like electricity, i.e., "data-power," if you will, than a trendy tech business. As a utility, it should command a premium valuation, reflective of its mission-critical nature and durable cash flows. It should not be valued as if it were a consumer credit business, whose cash flows may get cut in half in the next recession, possibly leading to the collapse of that consumer credit lender. AWS should be valued like a utility business whose cash flows are very durable.
  4. Our next best alternative (In the stock market, this is the yield on the 10-year and 2-year treasury bonds. If you can get a guaranteed 30% annual yield elsewhere, you should stop reading and go take advantage of that. But the 10-year is only 4.5% today, so, if that's your next best alternative, as it is mine, then stick with me). The 2-year has been trending down for years now, and the most recent inflation prints illustrated that we're well below the American long-run inflation rate average of 3%, and we're very nearly at the Fed's target of 2%. At the very least, yields should not be spiking, even with potential upward inflationary pressures from tariffs.

Let's now consider data that underpins the assumptions I shared with you above.

Starting with margins, below, we can see AWS' total sales:

AWS' Quarterly Sales Hit New Highs In Q1'25 (Now $117B Annualized)

AWS grew 17% year-over-year in Q1 and now sits at a $117 billion annualized revenue run rate. We continue to help organizations of all sizes accelerate their move to the cloud, helping to modernize their infrastructure, lower costs and speed up innovation.

Andy Jassy, Amazon Q1 '25 Earnings Call.

Below we can see AWS' operating income, with which, along with the above revenue metric, we can calculate operating and free cash flow margins today.

AWS' Operating Income (39% Operating Margin In Q1'25)

So AWS generates roughly 30% free cash flow margins today on $117B in annualized sales, and these sales are growing 17%, notably, AWS' operating margin has been steadily expanding for years now. In Q1'24, it was 37.5%. Over time, as AWS layers on more high-margin products, such as cybersecurity and AI products, margins should continue to expand, in the same way, Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) has been able to expand its margins over the years by upselling its existing customers on more and more products.

Microsoft's Operating Margin Expansion Over The Last 15 Years

Below, I shared a chart that further contextualizes AWS' growth:

AWS Growth Rate By Quarter Since Q1'24

As we can see, AWS' growth has hovered in the high teens range for the last year. The central question in determining the value of AWS, and, by extension Amazon, has become: How long can AWS sustain this growth?

As we've already covered, growth is an important component in how much a company is worth, so it's important that we identify how long AWS could grow in the high teens, and I believe, based on the data below, the runway for growth remains massive.

It's useful to remember that more than 85% of the global IT spend is still on premises, so not in the Cloud yet. It seems pretty straightforward to me that this equation will flip in the next 10 to 20 years.

Before this generation of AI, we thought AWS had the chance to ultimately be a multi-$100 billion revenue run rate business.

We now think it could be even larger.

If you believe your mission is to make customers' lives easier and better every day and you believe that every customer experience will be reinvented with AI, you're going to invest very aggressively in AI and that's what we're doing.

Andy Jassy, Amazon Q1 '25 Earnings Call (emphasis added).

Datadog, Inc. (DDOG) routinely echoes this data from CEO Andy Jassy in sharing the following charts in its quarterly reports:

Datadog's Depiction Of The Growth Of The Cloud Market

Datadog's Depiction Of The Growth Of The Cloud Market

Our AI business has a multibillion dollar annual revenue run rate, continues to grow triple-digit year-over-year percentages, and is still in its very early days. While there is good reason for the high optimism about AI, I conclude my AWS comments with the reminder that there's still so much on-premises infrastructure yet to be moved to the cloud. Infrastructure modernization is much less actually to talk about AI. The fundamental to any company's technology and invention capabilities to develop their productivity is speed and cost structure. If a company is to realize the full potential of AI, they are going to need their infrastructure and data in the cloud.

Andy Jassy, CEO, Amazon Q1 '25 Earnings Call (emphasis added).

At this point, we have all the data we need to value AWS:

  • $117B annualized revenue run rate.
  • ~40% operating margin; long run free cash flow margin of 35%, akin to Microsoft.
  • 10.0B shares outstanding.
  • 17% growth, with a huge runway still ahead, implying that 15%+ growth, on average, is likely for years to come.
  • Highly durable cash flows, implying free cash flow multiple should be elevated.
  • With all of this data in mind: (35% * $117B)/10.9B = $3.75 * 50x(p/fcf) = $188/share.

50x price to free cash flow might be considered expensive, but, for instance, Microsoft trades at 35.6x price to earnings with 13.3% growth. Microsoft trades at this premium valuation because it is known to be one of the most durable businesses on earth, as evidenced by its credit rating which is higher than the U.S. government.

For the growth and cash flow durability that we receive in buying AWS, I believe this is fair.

Additionally, it would be more than fair if AWS were a standalone business that was buying back shares routinely and making software-related acquisitions in a focused and concerted manner.

Imagine this scenario:

AWS did 35% FCF margins, grew 15%+ for many years to come, produced massive free cash flow, and bought back shares every year with that cash flow:

Solid growth that could actually accelerate in the years ahead, durable cash flows, sustained innovation, and a repurchase program that would help to accelerate growth.

And then, I would just say on how to think about relative growth rates, you always have to -- the year-over-year growth rate is really only a function of the percentage growth on the base with which you are operating from. And we just have a very meaningfully larger base on the technology infrastructure side than others. And so, when it's still thinking about 17% year-over-year growth on a $117 billion revenue run rate, it's still pretty significant growth. And as I said, I think we could be doing more, more if we had more capacity and I expect that the capacity to ease in the coming months.

Andy Jassy, Amazon Q1 '25 Earnings Call (emphasis added).

In such a scenario, AWS would grow earnings at a solid 20%+ clip, more than justifying the 50x multiple I applied above.

Ross Sandler: So, Andy, it seems like you've been bringing on a lot more P5 GPU instances since February from what it looks like to kind of support all these new AI workloads. So, how would you characterize in the first quarter and maybe here in the second quarter, the kind of supply/demand imbalance that you talked about before around AI workloads? And when do you think that AWS will be in a position to kind of capture enough AI revenue to drive acceleration? Is that something that could happen this year? Do you see that more like next year, given your capacity constraints? Thank you very much.

Andy Jassy: Thanks, Ross. I would say that we've been bringing on a lot of P5s, which is a form of NVIDIA chip instances, as well as landing more and more Trainium 2 instances as fast as we can. And I would tell you that our AI business right now is a multi-billion dollar annual run rate business that's growing triple-digit percentages year-over-year. And we, as fast as we actually put the capacity in, it's being consumed. So, I think we could be driving -- we could be helping more customers and driving more revenue for the business if we had more capacity. We have a lot more Trainium 2 instances, and the next generation of NVIDIA's instances landing in the coming months. I expect that there are other parts of the supply chain that are a little bit jammed up as well as motherboards and some other componentry. And some of that is just because there is so much demand right now, but I do believe that the supply chain issues and the capacity issues will continue to get better as the year proceeds.

Andy Jassy, CEO, Amazon Q1 '25 Earnings Call (emphasis added).

There's a solid chance we see AWS accelerate growth, which would even further the case that we're paying $2T today for AWS alone and getting the rest of the business for free.

The Curious Case Of Walmart's Valuation Premium

Amazon's valuation has always been one of the great wonders of the stock market. From the exuberant highs of the dot-com Bubble to the excessively depressed lows of the subsequent crash to the stock's tendency to trade, today, as if it were as fickle, early-stage growth stock, when it's arguably the scariest business on earth in terms of its dominance… we just never know what we're going to get.

To this end, there's been yet another instance of mind-boggling price and valuation dynamics underway for Amazon in recent quarters.

Below, I shared a price-to-earnings comparison between Amazon and Walmart, and, as we can see, Walmart trades at a price-to-earnings of 42x while Amazon trades at 33.4x.

Walmart P/E Ratio & Amazon P/E Ratio

Despite having more than 2x Walmart's growth, which I depicted in the chart below, and arguably one of the most dominant businesses in the history of humanity, "4%-growing-Walmart" trades at a 30% premium to Amazon.

Amazon & Walmart Growth Rates

If we factor in the highly probable margin expansion Amazon will continue to experience in the quarters and years ahead, it's even more awe-inspiring.

Amazon TTM Operating Margin

Amazon Quarterly Operating Margin

So we're likely looking at something more like a 100% valuation premium for Walmart in comparison to Amazon today, using Amazon's fully matured margins…

It's genuinely mind-boggling, especially considering Amazon's subscription business component for its ecommerce business, i.e., Prime, makes its cash flows utility-like in nature.

I discussed this important idea here recently.

Explaining The 8th Wonder Of The World (Amazon's Valuation)

I've posited explanations for why the market would trade Amazon in this fashion in the past.

I believe the primary reason the market continues to trade Amazon at such a massive discount is that the market believes that Amazon's P&L, i.e., OpEx and R&D, are so excessively bloated that huge chunks of shareholder capital are being burned each year.

The market's discount on Amazon is a de facto punishment for inefficient use of energy (shareholder capital/money).

I have often shared that the market is an evolutionary mechanism meant to strengthen the human species, and this, according to my speculative narrative, would align with that idea.

Concretely, Amazon's inability to grow without further dilution, i.e., it continues to dilute shareholders even with the massive cash resources it generates each year, is a prime reason for the above-illustrated valuation dynamic.

While Amazon keeps diluting shareholders, despite staggering amounts of annual gross profit generation, Walmart steadily reduces shares outstanding and reliably grows free cash flow per share each year.

All this said, I do believe that:

  1. Amazon's ecommerce business is worth more than $0. The market currently believes it is worth $0. I think this will be re-litigated in the quarters ahead, leading to a substantial valuation re-rating upward for Amazon.
  2. Amazon's operating margin will continue to expand as AWS and ads become larger portions of the business, making its current 33.4x price-to-earnings ratio even lower than it currently appears.
  3. Amazon will eventually determine that it could be more innovative and grow faster if it implemented a share buyback program that instilled fiscal discipline in the company at its current scale.

If all of these factors fall into place, and I am very confident they will, Amazon will likely trade to $4T in market cap rather quickly, and reach $400/share+ in the next 3-5 years.

MACS Update

Since the inception of the LASR Index, I have been running a strategy within it entitled "MACS."

MACS, Ex AMZN & Its 8th Wonder Valuation, Have Done Great YTD

MACS stands for MercadoLibre, Inc. (MELI), Amazon, Coupang, Inc. (CPNG), and Sea Limited (SE), and it is meant to capitalize on the meteoric rise of digital ad spend on these platforms, which I've been tracking since Amazon did $2.5B in quarterly ads revenue in 2018.

Today, Amazon's ads business is far, far bigger, and, in Q3'24 (compared to the $2.5B number above), it generated $14.3B in ad revenue. A scaling event in just 6 years, which, as an aside, Meli, Sea Ltd., and Coupang are presently experiencing as well, though the market has vehemently fought this notion for years now.

Amazon's Ads Business Quarterly Revenue

We are pleased with the strong growth on a very large base generating $13.9 billion of revenue in the quarter and growing 19% year-over-year. We're seeing strength across our broad portfolio of full-funnel advertising offerings that help advertisers reach an average ad-supported audience of more than 275 million in the U.S. alone. This includes our top of funnel efforts to drive brand awareness to bottom-of-funnel offerings where we measure outcomes at the point of conversion.

Andy Jassy, Amazon Q1 '25 Earnings Call.

To close, I am excited to be able to buy AWS for roughly $2T. I believe it will be worth much more over time, and I do believe Amazon will be a $400/share stock in the next 3-5 years as it further expands its margins, begins generating $100B+ in free cash flow, and initiates a buyback program that will further accelerate earnings growth.

data.title: NBB: An Oddity, But Possibly Not Worth It
data.description:
Some ideas are a little odd

Or, perhaps I should weaken that. Some ideas first strike me as a little odd. The big thing about municipal bonds is that they are Federal income tax-free. So, deliberately going out and investing in municipal bonds that are not Fed income tax-free seems odd. But then it's possible that there's a wrinkle in there which makes it not so odd.

But, still – we must understand what people are trying to do inside an instrument before we can decide whether we want to use that instrument. The design is going to influence, if not determine, how the instrument then performs and what it performs. We then get to match that up against what we hope to achieve and only put our money where there is at least the possibility of a congruence there.

Well, that's the aim anyway.

Nuveen Taxable Municipal Income Fund (NYSE:NBB)

This is a bond fund – not an ETF. As such, it's a closed-end fund. This has an advantage, in that it cannot be subject to a redemption cascade – that's where we all turn up demanding our money back at the same time, everything is disposed of in a fire sale, and we all lose a fortune because of those fire sale prices. Yes, I know, possibly only a mild risk, but it has happened to at least two open-ended funds in commercial property.

On the other hand, a closed-end fund can wander from net asset value:

That's not too bad, I've seen wider than that. Note that it can be variable, though.

As we all know, there are three types of bond: Treasury, Corporate, and Municipal. Lending to the Feds, companies, and sub-national governmental bodies. Lending to the Feds has no credit risk. Well, sort of – national governments have gone bust before now, but not the US one for a couple of centuries. Also, we all think that if they do, then we've all got far larger problems than financial market returns. So, Treasuries are "credit risk-free".

Treasuries do face interest rate risk. If base rates (say, the Fed Fund Rate) change, then we can buy new bonds at a 4% coupon instead of the 3% of yesterday. Well, say that's the interest rate change but the point holds, up or down, for any change in base rates. So, the 4% on $100 makes that bond worth more than the old 3% on $100 one. How much more or less? Well, by the amount of interest foregone by having the old, not the new. That then depends on the life of the bond – the tenor. The capital value of that $1 a year on $100 is greater over 30 years than it is over 30 days. Obviously.

So, long bonds change capital value more than short tenor ones when interest rates change. Sadly, it's also true that coupons – the interest rate – rise the longer a loan or bond is for. So, to gain income, we have to move out in tenor, which exposes us to more interest rate risk. Or, we can run that the other way around, we get paid for carrying risk in financial markets; therefore the coupon is higher because the interest rate risk is higher.

That's just the way it works

Corporates and munis face interest rate risk, but also credit risk – the idea that someone might go bust and not repay. True, in munis, this is pretty low – I've seen an estimate of 0.1% – but it is still there. Credit risk means that we don't want to be all eggs in one basket. So, diversification is spread across many bond issues and many issuers.

This then gives us a problem as while we can usually buy bonds at issue – and have to hold them to redemption – in $1,000 lots to trade in secondary markets can require much larger holdings. The European corporate bond markets insist upon a $200k par value as the minimum trade.

So, generally, we should gain our diversification through investing into a bond fund.

Another difference between open and closed-end funds is that a CEF will often leverage up. As here, to about 40%. The cost of the interest burden is lower – well, that's the point at least – than the coupons being received, thus juicing returns to investors.

As we can see here:

About 40% leverage, interest costs are – just – below coupon, etc. That earnings distribution ratio is a little odd and is what decides me against this fund. Or rather, puzzles me as to who would benefit from it to make a weaker statement. We'll come to that.

Tax is the normal big muni advantage

Interest on most munis is not subject to the Federal income tax. That's the big advantage of them. That also means that munis have to pay a lower coupon in order to attract investors – it is the cash in hand at the end of the day that drives investment decisions, after all.

But there are some munis that are subject to income tax – private purpose bonds. So, say, a stadium financing – just as an example. The municipality might issue, and guarantee even, the bond, but the real beneficiary is the stadium owner/team. So, no tax break.

As private-purpose bonds don't get the tax break, they have to pay higher coupons. And so it's wholly reasonable to invest in that universe. They're far more like corporate bonds – taxable, credit and interest rate risks, and so on. So, why not a fund that does that? As here.

NBB positions

We can see what the fund is invested in:

We can also see a full list here.

We can gain an idea of tenor here:

So, they're out in the longer end of the bond market looking for income. They're also in taxable munis which are again looking for income. OK, fine, a strategy, why not?

Here's the bit I don't get

Distributions are currently estimated to include the following amounts from sources other than net investment income: 0% capital gains and 21% return of capital.

Umm, why?

Why would we want to be in a bond fund which is returning capital to us? That's the little bit of all of this that I'm not understanding. We could be in NVG and gain a higher income with a greater portion of capital being returned as income if we wished. It's the idea of having the capital returned that puzzles.

Now, of course, it could be that I'm just not understanding something here. Despite my usual claims of omniscience, this does, in fact, happen. But my usual reaction to not grasping the logic of a financial instrument is to go on to insist that the logic isn't there. Why do we want capital returned?

Why am I wrong?

Of course, it's possible I'm wrong. The most likely reason I am is because I'm not grasping some piece – some subtle piece, perhaps – of the US taxation system.

But other than that no, the fund does what I and it, the fund, say it does. Invests, at fairly long tenor, in taxable munis. These produce a perfectly decent income stream at what is likely to be low credit risk default rates – at that cost of carrying interest rate risk, of course.

My opinion

Conversion of capital into a distribution stream just doesn't make sense to me. Therefore, I'm wary of this fund. The base idea, taxable munis, leverage, closed-end fund, long tenor, that's all fine. As long as we understand all of those things, then that's the trade-offs we make. But that capital return is the one thing that makes me think, well, no. Not because it is clearly and obviously wrong, but because I cannot think of who that would be beneficial to. Therefore, I assume that it's not beneficial.

data.title: Private Credit's Surge Has Investors Excited And Regulators Concerned
data.description:
By Pete Vatev, CFA, FRM

Private credit has rapidly evolved from a niche asset class into a dominant force in the global lending ecosystem, now representing an estimated $2.5 trillion industry[1] rivaling traditional bank lending and public debt markets. For institutional investors navigating a shifting macroeconomic and regulatory landscape, the asset class presents both compelling opportunities and growing concerns.

While private credit promises bespoke deal structures, superior yields, and diversification away from traditional fixed income, its accelerated growth — fueled by bank retrenchment and heightened investor appetite — raises critical questions about liquidity, transparency, and systemic risk.

This transformation has been driven by structural shifts in the financial system. Chief among them: tighter post-2008 banking regulations, the persistent search for yield in low-interest rate environments, and the growing demand from private equity for more flexible, non-traditional sources of financing.

Drivers of Private Credit Growth

Several key factors have contributed to the rise of private credit:

  • Banking Regulation & Retrenchment: Post-2008 financial reforms, such as Basel III and Dodd-Frank, imposed stricter capital requirements on banks, limiting their ability to lend to middle-market firms[2]. Private credit funds stepped in to fill this gap.
  • Investor Demand for Yield: In a low-interest-rate environment, institutional investors, including pension funds and insurers, sought higher returns through private credit investments.[3]
  • Private Equity Expansion: The growth of private equity has fueled demand for direct lending, as firms prefer tailored financing solutions over traditional syndicated loans.[4]
  • Flexibility & Speed: Private credit offers customized loan structures, faster execution, and less regulatory oversight, making it attractive to borrowers.[5]

Implications for Financial Stability and Systemic Risk

Despite its benefits, private credit introduces new vulnerabilities to the financial system:

  • Liquidity Risks: Unlike banks, private credit funds lack access to central bank liquidity. Even though many funds restrict investor withdrawals to quarterly or annual redemption windows, during economic downturns when borrower defaults rise and secondary market liquidity dries up, investor redemption demands could trigger fire sales and market instability.
  • Leverage & Concentration: Many private credit funds operate with high leverage, amplifying returns but also increasing fragility. Business Development Companies (BDCs), for example, were allowed to increase their leverage cap to 2:1 in 2018[6], raising concerns about systemic risk.
  • Opaque Valuations: Private credit assets are not publicly traded, making valuations less transparent and potentially stale, which could mask underlying risks.[7]
  • Interlinkages with Banks: While private credit operates outside traditional banking, its growing ties to bank funding could create contagion risks in a downturn.[8]

Regulatory Outlook

Regulators, including the Federal Reserve, the International Monetary Fund (IMF), and the Bank for International Settlements (BIS), are increasingly scrutinizing private credit’s role in financial markets. The IMF warns that private credit’s expansion could amplify economic shocks, particularly if underwriting standards deteriorate. The BIS highlights the need for greater transparency and risk monitoring, especially as retail investors gain exposure to the asset class.

More to Think About

For allocators and asset owners, private credit represents a strategic lever in pursuit of yield and portfolio diversification. But as capital continues to pour into the space, often outpacing risk infrastructure, the investment thesis must be continually reexamined through a risk-adjusted lens. With increasing scrutiny from global regulators and the growing complexity of credit markets, due diligence and scenario planning will be essential to avoid hidden vulnerabilities and ensure resilience in the next phase of the credit cycle.

At the same time, policymakers are increasingly alert to the broader financial implications of private credit’s ascent. Global regulators including the Federal Reserve, IMF, and BIS have warned that unchecked growth in opaque, illiquid segments of credit markets could amplify shocks and create feedback loops across institutions. Notably, the growing accessibility of private credit products to retail investors, often via interval funds and public BDCs, raises further concerns about liquidity mismatches and valuation transparency. These dynamics are likely to draw heightened regulatory attention as retail participation expands.

Striking the right balance between market innovation and systemic oversight will be crucial not just for regulators but for institutional investors who must navigate these crosscurrents with discipline and foresight.

[1] Bank for International Settlements (BIS) Private Credit Market Overview, 2025.

[2] Federal Reserve Report on Private Credit Characteristics and Risks, 2024.

[3] IMF Global Financial Stability Report, April 2024.

[4] IMF Blog on Private Credit Growth, 2024.

[5] What is private credit, Brookings, 2024.

[6] H.R.4267 – Small Business Credit Availability Act, 2018

[7] Federal Reserve Report on Private Credit Characteristics and Risks, 2024.

[8] Bank Lending to Private Equity and Private Credit Funds: Insights from Regulatory Data, Fed Boston 2025

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Mixed Data For May Clouds Outlook For U.S. Economy
data.description:
May is shaping up to as challenging month for reading the macro tea leaves in an effort to gauge how tariffs are affecting the US economy. Tomorrow’s payrolls report from the Labor Department may provide clarity. Meanwhile, the early profile for last month is a study in contrasts.

Let’s start with the good news, based on the Atlanta Fed’s current nowcast (as of June 2) for the upcoming second-quarter report on GDP. The bank’s GDPNow model points to a sizzling rebound in growth for the April-through-June quarter: +4.6% vs. -0.2% reported for Q1. That’s an extraordinarily strong gain and so it will be useful to see if it holds up in today’s revision.

One reason to be cautious on expecting a blowout increase: the New York Fed’s current nowcast (May 30) indicates a much softer, albeit still-solid rebound for Q2: +2.4%.

Another reason for reserving judgment: yesterday’s surprisingly weak ISM Services Index report for May. The crucial services sector slipped to a slight contraction last month. The index dropped to 49.4 last month, just below the neutral 50 mark, easing to the lowest level since June 2024.

A competing survey of the services sector for last month paints a brighter profile via the S&P Global US Services PMI, which posted a firmer pace of moderate growth in May. “That said, the improvements come from a low base, following a very gloomy April, which saw growth nearly stall as confidence sank to a two-and-half year low,” said Chris Williamson, chief business economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence.

S&P Global’s US Composite PMI, a GDP proxy, also shows a pickup in business activity last month. This index accelerated sharply after April’s 19-month low, despite weakness in the manufacturing sector.

The ADP Employment Report for May, by comparison, is ringing a warning bell. This estimate shows that hiring at US companies slowed to a crawl last month. Payrolls rose just 37,000 last month, far below expectations and the lowest increase since March 2023. “After a strong start to the year, hiring is losing momentum,” said Nela Richardson, chief economist for ADP.

ADP’s track record for anticipating the official payrolls data from the Labor Department is mixed, at best, and so it’s debatable if the weak print is a reliable warning sign for the labor market. “There is no correlation between the ADP and BLS employment report,” points out Advisor Perspectives.

Economists are expecting a downshift in private-sector hiring in tomorrow’s report from the government. The consensus forecast (via Econoday.com) indicates that companies hired 123,000 workers last month. That’s down from 167,000, but the slowdown is less worrisome than the virtual collapse indicated by the ADP report.

The margin for error is thin in the current climate. Perhaps the only clarity at the moment is that a much weaker-than-expected payrolls report from the government tomorrow will confirm the worst fears about the blowback from a global trade war.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Real Reason You Should Buy Alphabet
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Alphabet Inc. (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG) appears to be somewhat undervalued when compared to some of its peers.

However, once we break down the worth of the Google Search business as a separate entity, it actually seems like the rest of the business is trading at a fair multiple.

From a conservative standpoint, Google is fairly valued.

However, with all the opportunities for growth acceleration presented by YouTube, Cloud, and Waymo, this seems like a no-brainer.

Google Search Vs. The Rest Of The Business

In this article, I’m going to break down Google by Search and its other businesses in order to gain a better insight into the valuation.

We’ll take the full 2024 numbers for this exercise.

Google Search & Other generated $198 billion in revenues. That represents 65% of the total Google Services revenue.

In turn, Google Services generated $121,263 million. For simplicity’s sake, let’s say Google Search was also 65% of this income.

That leaves us with $78 billion in earnings attributable to Google Search and $34 billion attributable to the rest of the business.

In other words, close to 65% of operating income came from Search, but that still leaves a good amount coming from the other segments.

Google Search Is Worth At Least One Trillion

If we were to discount the Search business altogether, then the rest of the business would be trading at a PE of almost 60. Pretty rich, but some might say not absurd in today’s market.

However, obviously, Search has some value, and I’d say at the low end it’s worth $1 trillion.

I’m getting this by applying a 15x multiple to the income of $78 billion. This is in line with the EV/EBITDA of similar companies like Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT).

It’s, in fact, on the low end, but I’m making a conservative estimate based on the idea that Search is somehow doomed because of AI, though there is no evidence of this yet.

30 PE On Rest Of The Business Seems Reasonable

Google’s market cap is around $2 trillion.

If $1 trillion in market cap can be attributed to Search, then in order for the rest of the business to match today's total market cap ($2 trillion), it would have to be worth around $1 trillion too.

The rest of the business, by our prior calculations, is generating $34 billion in operating income, so applying a PE of around 30 would get us to the $1 trillion valuation.

This is for a business growing revenues at around 15% on aggregate, by my calculations.

A 30 PE sits just in between the valuation for Meta and Microsoft, which are the two companies we compared Google to before.

Is Google Fairly Valued?

There’s an argument to be made, therefore, that at a market cap of $2 trillion, Google is actually quite fairly valued.

However, this is, in my opinion, a very conservative valuation. It assumes Search is a business that deserves a discount when, for the moment, evidence suggests this could carry a much larger multiple.

It’s a largely profitable business with a moat and double-digit revenue growth. Without the AI threat, Search itself could easily be valued at $2 trillion, in my opinion, leaving a lot of room for upside.

But even in a worst-case scenario, Google’s minimum valuation sits somewhere close to $2 trillion, allowing us to buy three businesses whose growth I expect will dramatically accelerate without too much downside.

Triple Growth Threat

Cloud grew 28% YoY in Q1 of 2025, and generated an operating income of $2.2 billion. This market should easily grow at 20% for the next 5 years. The compounding effects once you apply them to the high margin are staggering.

Cloud will easily be bringing in $6 billion yearly by 2030.

YouTube grew close to 15% in 2024, and it is quickly becoming a powerhouse in the entertainment ecosystem. Global streaming is expected to grow at 18% until 2030.

And Waymo is arguably the only successful self-driving company in the market right now:

It’s expected that Waymo could bring in $180 million in revenues in 2025, double last year's.

Again, this is arguably the market leader in the AV ride market, which Statista believes will reach a size of $2.3 trillion by 2030.

A Growth Investor’s Dream

In Google, I see a company with little to no downside and a perfect set-up for its three high-growth businesses.

For a growth investor, you can buy Google and receive all the benefits of investing in high-growth businesses while also having:

  • A rock-solid balance sheet
  • Strong profitability
  • Size and deep pockets to leverage

All the upsides to growth investing with limited downside.

Well, not all the upside, as Google’s share price won’t explode in the way a smaller cap company that is just starting out could.

Risks

Although there's limited downside in my view, there are also plenty of potential risk factors.

Cloud is a highly competitive market that could eventually be dominated by only a few companies. Google looks to be one of them, but it could lose its dominance.

Waymo is also a highly speculative bet, and even if it does generate revenues, it will likely lose money for some time.

Lastly, it's still unclear what will happen with Search. It looks like, for now, the effects of AI have not been too negative, but this could accelerate as younger people begin to dominate the internet.

Final Thoughts

So in a way, I agree with both sides.

Google is fairly valued in terms of today’s metrics, making it at least a hold. However, once you account for all the upside potential in the businesses outside of Search, this becomes a no-brainer buy.

data.title: Gabelli Pet Parents' Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
STRATEGY OVERVIEW

The Gabelli Pet Parents' Fund seeks to provide capital appreciation. Under normal market conditions, the Fund invests at least 80% of its net assets in common and preferred shares of publicly traded domestic and foreign companies of all capitalization ranges in the pet industry. The pet industry includes companies that offer services and products for pets and pet owners ("Pet Parents"). The Fund is non-diversified.

INVESTMENT SCORECARD

The Gabelli Pet Parents Fund (MUTF:PETZX) was down 7.28% in Q1 2025. The largest contributors in the quarter were CVS Health Corp. (CVS), Vetoquinol S.A. (OTCPK:VETOF), and Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY), which returned 52.79%, 7.94%, and 22.21% respectively.

While pet related stocks pulled back in Q1, our outlook for the industry remains positive. We anticipate 5%-6% growth in the pet economy in 2025, bringing the market to $380 billion. This will largely be through growth in pet healthcare, premium nutrition, and innovative pet technologies. Technological integration remains a critical driver of industry expansion. The proliferation of pet tech—including wearable health trackers, smart feeding solutions, and telemedicine platforms—is creating entirely new market segments.

Our investment strategy focuses on companies at the forefront of these innovations, ensuring we capture value from technological disruption. Approximately 70% of U.S. households feature animal companions, and the non-discretionary nature of pet-related expenditure continues to provide a buffer against economic fluctuations, offering stable revenue streams for companies operating in this space. We believe the Fund is well positioned and that active management, coupled with overall industry growth, provides an excellent opportunity for short- and long-term value creation.

Investments in foreign instruments or currencies can involve greater risks and volatility than U.S. investments because of adverse market, economic, political, regulatory, geopolitical or other conditions.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

CVS Health Corp. (5.7% of net assets as of March 31, 2025) (CVS - $67.75 - NYSE) is a leading healthcare company that provides a wide range of services, including retail pharmacy operations, pharmacy benefit management through CVS Caremark, and health insurance through Aetna. The company is a large retailer of pet food and vaccinations. The company published positive guidance for 2025, with $5.75 to $6.00 of EPS and $6.5 billion of operating cash flow. We have a PMV of $83 for CVS.

Vetoquinol S.A. (2.5%) (VETO - $83.80 / €77.80 - LSE) is a global animal health company specializing in the development, production, and marketing of veterinary pharmaceuticals and health supplements for pets and livestock. The company collaborates closely with veterinarians to provide innovative solutions for animal care, including products for dermatology, dental care, and nutritional support. Notable offerings include Phovia, a light therapy solution for faster skin recovery in pets, and Zylkene, a calming supplement for dogs and cats. The company generated significant cash flow in 2024 through margin expansion and positive product mix. We have a PMV of €85 for Vetoquinol.

Nestlé (1.8%) (NESN.SW - $100.98 /CHF 89.34 - SIX) is a Swiss multinational food and drink processing conglomerate that operates globally, offering a diverse range of products including coffee, bottled water, infant formula, and frozen foods. The company is renowned for its extensive portfolio of brands such as Nescafé, Kit Kat, and Maggi, and it also has a significant presence in the pet care market through its subsidiary Nestlé Purina PetCare. The company produced strong cash flows in 2024 and guidance remains unchanged for 2025 despite commodity price movements. We have a PMV of CHF 96 for Nestlé using a sum-of-the-parts methodology.

PORTFOLIO HIGHLIGHTS

Total Net Assets: $3.4 Million
NAV: $13.37
Turnover: (A) 8%
Inception Date: 06/19/18
Gross/Net Expense Ratio: (B) 6.07%/0.92%
SHARE CLASS SYMBOL
Class I: PETZX
Class A: PETGX

(A) For the twelve months ended September 30, 2024.

(B) As of the current prospectus dated January 28, 2025. Net expense ratio after reimbursement from the Adviser. Effective through January 28, 2026, unless terminated early by the Fund's Board of Directors.

COMPARATIVE RESULTS

Gabelli Pet Parents' Fund QTR 1 Year 3 Year 5 Year Since Inception (06/19/18) (C)
Class I (PETZX) (7.28)% 6.64% (0.98)% 10.46% 6.02%
Class A (MUTF:PETGX) (7.30)% 6.34% (1.23)% 10.29% 5.90%
S&P 500 Index (D) (4.27) 8.25 9.06 18.59 12.88

Average Annual Returns through March 31, 2025 (a)(b) Total returns and average annual returns reflect changes in share price, reinvestment of distributions, and are net of expenses. Performance returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized.

(A) The Fund's fiscal year ends on September 30.

(B) Returns would have been lower had Gabelli Funds, LLC, (the "Adviser") not reimbursed certain expenses of the Fund.

(C) Performance prior to the commencement of operations on April 1, 2019, is from the Predecessor Fund, Gabelli Pet Parents' NextShares.

(D) The S&P 500 Index is a market capitalization weighted index of 500 large capitalization stocks commonly used to represent the U.S. equity market. Dividends are considered reinvested. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses of the Fund before investing. The prospectuses contain information about these and other matters and should be read carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus, please visit our website at Home.

Returns represent past performance and do not guarantee future results. Investment returns and the principal value of an investment will fluctuate. When shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data presented. Visit Home for performance information as of the most recent month end.

Funds concentrating in specific sectors may experience greater fluctuations in value than funds that are more diversified. Investing in foreign securities involves risks not ordinarily associated with investments in domestic issues including currency fluctuations, economic and political risks.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*
Zoetis Inc. (ZTS) 9.6%
Chewy Inc. (CHWY) 9.5
Freshpet Inc. (FRPT) 6.5
IDEXX Laboratories Inc. (IDXX) 6.1
CVS Group plc (OTCPK:CVSGF) 5.7
Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN) 5.6

Elanco Animal Health Inc. (ELAN) 4.6
Petco Health & Wellness Co. Inc. (WOOF) 4.0
Qurate Retail Inc. (QVCGA) 3.4
Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

Daniel M. Miller

data.title: The Toro Company 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by The Toro Company in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.
